file.content:
CC
Learn Claude Code
  • Foundations
  • Context
  • Commands
  • Hooks
  • SDK
  • Cheatsheet
Interactive Study Guide

Master Claude Code
from Scratch

Everything you need to know — from what a coding assistant is, to custom hooks and the SDK. Verified, organized, and built for focused learning.

14Lessons
3.9×Real perf gain
∞Extensibility
Curriculum
  • 01 What is a Coding Assistant?
  • 02 What is Claude Code?
  • 03 How Tool Use Works
  • 04 Claude Code in Action
  • 05 Adding Context
  • 06 Making Changes & Power Modes
  • 07 Controlling Context
  • 08 Custom Commands
  • 09 MCP Servers
  • 10 GitHub Integration
  • 11 Hooks — Automating Quality
  • 12 Gotchas Around Hooks
  • 13 Useful Hooks — Real Examples
  • 14 The Claude Code SDK
01

What is a Coding Assistant?

A coding assistant is a tool that sits between you and a language model (like Claude), helping you write, fix, and manage code. It can read your files, run your tests, and make changes — all from a single conversation.

The Core Workflow

You send a task
"Fix the login bug described in this error message"
Assistant wraps the request
The coding assistant layer adds tool instructions to your message
Model gathers context
Reads relevant files, understands the codebase structure
Plan is formulated
A step-by-step approach is created before any changes
Action is taken
Files are edited, tests run, and results returned to you

Imagine hiring a freelance developer. You email them: "our checkout page is broken." A good developer doesn't guess — they look at the code, reproduce the issue, trace the logic, then fix it. A coding assistant works the same way, but faster.

Key Limitation

Language models can only process text input and produce text output. They cannot directly read files, run commands, or interact with external systems. This is why tool use is essential.

02

What is Claude Code?

Claude Code is Anthropic's official coding assistant built on the Claude language model. You interact with it through the terminal, and it can read files, write code, run commands, and connect to external services.

Why Claude Code?

FeatureWhat It Means for You
Superior tool useExceptionally good at knowing which tool to use and when — better than competing models
Extensible by designPlug in new tools (browsers, databases, APIs) as your needs grow via MCP servers
Better securitySearches your code directly — doesn't send your entire codebase to external indexing servers
Complex task handlingChains multiple tools together to solve multi-step problems effectively
03

How Tool Use Works

This is the most important concept to internalize. Language models can only process text. They cannot open a file, run a command, or check a website on their own. Tool use is the mechanism that bridges this gap.

Step-by-Step Breakdown

// 1. You ask:
"What's in my package.json?"

// 2. Claude Code adds instructions to the request:
"If you need to read a file, respond with: READ_FILE: <filename>"

// 3. The language model responds:
READ_FILE: package.json

// 4. Claude Code (assistant layer) actually reads the file

// 5. File contents are sent back to the language model

// 6. Claude reads the content and gives you a real answer

Think of a surgeon and a scrub nurse. The surgeon (language model) says "scalpel" and the nurse (Claude Code) physically hands it over. The surgeon has the knowledge; the nurse does the physical work. Together they complete the operation.

Remember

All language models require tool use for non-text tasks. The quality of tool use directly impacts how effective a coding assistant is. Claude's strength here is what makes Claude Code powerful.

04

Claude Code in Action

Example 1 — Performance Optimization

Claude analyzed the Chalk JavaScript library (~430M weekly downloads) and achieved a 3.9× throughput improvement:

Benchmark
Runs baseline performance benchmarks to measure current speed
Profile
Uses profiling tools to find the slowest code paths
Plan
Creates a prioritized to-do list of bottlenecks to fix
Fix & Verify
Implements changes and re-runs benchmarks to confirm improvement

Example 2 — PII Security Detection

Auto-detected in PR Review

A developer adds user email to a Lambda function output. Claude Code, running on GitHub Actions, reads the Terraform infrastructure, traces data flow, detects that emails now reach an external S3 bucket shared with a partner, and posts a warning before merge.

Example 3 — Data Analysis

Point Claude at a CSV of customer data. It opens a Jupyter notebook, writes and runs cells iteratively, identifies churn patterns, and produces charts — all in one conversation.

✓ Check Your Understanding
05

Adding Context

Claude works best when it understands your project. Too little context gives wrong answers. Too much irrelevant context makes it slower and less accurate. The goal is just enough.

The /init Command

/init

Scans your entire codebase, understands the architecture, and creates a Claude.md file. This file is automatically included in every future request.

Three Types of Claude.md

TypeCommitted?Use Case
Project level✓ YesArchitecture, coding standards, key files — shared with team
Local level✗ NoPersonal preferences, notes just for you
Machine level✗ NoGlobal preferences across all your projects

Example Claude.md

## Project: E-commerce Platform
- Database schema → /db/schema.sql (always reference for DB questions)
- We use PostgreSQL, not MySQL
- Auth handled by /src/auth/middleware.ts
- Never modify files in /vendor/

The @ Symbol — Pinpoint Context

@src/auth/middleware.ts — why is the login token expiring early?

Mention files directly instead of letting Claude search. It's like handing a new employee the exact page they need instead of saying "the answer is somewhere in the wiki."

The # Symbol — Memory Mode

# Always use async/await instead of .then() chains

Immediately updates your Claude.md so Claude remembers this in all future interactions.

06

Making Changes & Power Modes

Pasting Screenshots — Ctrl+V

⚠ Mac Gotcha

Use Control+V, not Command+V. Command+V does not paste screenshots into Claude Code on macOS.

Screenshot a broken UI element, paste it, and say "fix the alignment here." No need to describe what you see in words.

Plan Mode — For Breadth

Activate with Shift + Tab (twice). Claude will research all relevant files, build a detailed plan, and only then start making changes. Ideal for multi-file features and refactoring.

Thinking Mode — For Depth

Include phrases like "Ultra think" in your message. Gives Claude an extended reasoning budget for complex logic and subtle bugs.

Ultra think — why does our payment processing occasionally fail
for orders placed exactly at midnight?

When to Use Each Mode

ModeHandlesBest For
Plan ModeBreadthMulti-step tasks, wide codebase understanding
Thinking ModeDepthTricky logic, debugging specific issues
Both combinedBreadth + DepthThe most complex tasks you face
Cost Note

Both modes consume additional tokens. Use them strategically rather than on every request.

Git Integration

Claude Code can stage files, create commits, and write descriptive commit messages automatically. Just say "commit the changes you just made."

07

Controlling Context

Long conversations accumulate noise that can confuse Claude. These tools keep you focused:

Escape ×1
Stop Claude mid-response and redirect
Escape + #
Stop, then add permanent memory to prevent repeated mistakes
Escape ×2
Rewind: jump back to any earlier message
/compact
Summarize conversation, preserve knowledge, clear clutter
/clear
Wipe entire conversation — fresh start for a new task
Pro Tip

Use Double Escape when you've gone down a debugging rabbit hole for 10+ messages with no results. Jump back to before the rabbit hole and try a different approach — without losing relevant context.

08

Custom Commands

Custom commands let you create your own slash commands to automate repetitive tasks.

How to Create One

Create folder
.claude/commands/ in your project root
Create a markdown file
Filename becomes the command name: audit.md → /audit
Write instructions
Plain English instructions Claude will follow when you invoke the command
Restart Claude Code
Required to activate new command files

Example — /audit Command

Audit all npm dependencies in package.json for known vulnerabilities.
For each vulnerable package:
1. List the package name and current version
2. Describe the vulnerability
3. Suggest the safest upgrade path

Commands with Arguments

Generate comprehensive unit tests for the file at: $arguments

Include tests for:
- Happy path
- Edge cases
- Error handling
/test src/components/Button.tsx
09

MCP Servers — Extending Claude's Abilities

MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers are external tools you plug into Claude Code to give it new capabilities. Think of them as "apps" Claude can use — browsers, databases, design tools, and more.

Installing an MCP Server

# Install the Playwright browser automation server
claude mcp add playwright npx @playwright/mcp

Auto-Approving MCP Tools

{
  "allow": ["MCP__playwright"]
}

Real World — Automated UI Refinement

1→Opens your app at localhost:3000 in a real browser
2→Takes a screenshot, analyzes UI quality
3→Edits the component code to improve styling
4→Takes another screenshot, compares to the first
5→Iterates automatically until satisfied — no human needed
10

GitHub Integration

Claude Code can run directly inside GitHub Actions, triggered by pull requests and issues.

Setup

/install-github-app

This auto-creates two GitHub Actions in your repo:

ActionWhat It Does
Mention supportTag @claude in any issue or PR comment to assign Claude a task
PR reviewClaude automatically reviews every new pull request
Important: Permissions

You must explicitly list all permissions for Claude Code in your Actions config. MCP server tools require individual permission listing — there are no shortcuts.

11

Hooks — Automating Quality Control

Hooks are custom scripts that run automatically before or after Claude uses a tool. They let you enforce rules, run checks, and provide automated feedback.

The Two Hook Types

TypeWhen It RunsCan Block?
Pre-tool useBefore Claude executes a tool✓ Yes (exit 2)
Post-tool useAfter Claude executes a tool✗ No

How a Hook Decision Works

Claude→Wants to read a file
Hook→Your pre-tool hook script runs first
Check→Is this file .env?
YES→exit(2) — Claude is blocked + gets your error message via stderr
NO→exit(0) — Claude proceeds normally

Configuration

{
  "hooks": {
    "preToolUse": [
      {
        "matcher": "read|grep",
        "command": "node /absolute/path/to/hooks/read_hook.js"
      }
    ]
  }
}

Hook Script Example

const chunks = [];

process.stdin.on('data', chunk => chunks.push(chunk));
process.stdin.on('end', () => {
  const data = JSON.parse(Buffer.concat(chunks).toString());
  const filePath = data.tool_input?.path || '';

  if (filePath.includes('.env')) {
    console.error('Blocked: .env files are off-limits for security');
    process.exit(2); // Block the tool call
  }

  process.exit(0); // Allow the tool call
});
How Data Flows

The hook receives a JSON object via stdin containing: session_id, tool_name (e.g., "read" or "grep"), and tool_input (including the file path). Your script parses this and exits with the appropriate code.

12

Gotchas Around Hooks

The Absolute Path Problem

Claude Code recommends absolute paths in hook commands for security (prevents path interception attacks). But absolute paths are machine-specific, making it hard to share settings with your team.

The Problem

Your machine: /Users/alice/myproject/hooks/read_hook.js
Colleague's machine: /Users/bob/work/myproject/hooks/read_hook.js
Committing your path breaks it for everyone else.

The Solution — Template + Setup Script

Create a settings.example.json template with a $PWD placeholder:

{
  "hooks": {
    "preToolUse": [{
      "matcher": "read|grep",
      "command": "node $PWD/hooks/read_hook.js"
    }]
  }
}

A scripts/init-claude.js setup script replaces $PWD with each developer's actual project path when they run:

npm run setup   # installs deps + generates settings.local.json
Result

Commit settings.example.json to git. Add settings.local.json to .gitignore. Every developer gets correct absolute paths automatically.

Remember

You must restart Claude Code after any hook configuration changes for them to take effect.

13

Useful Hooks — Real Examples

Hook 1 — TypeScript Type Checker

The problem: Claude edits a function's signature but forgets to update all call sites, leaving type errors scattered in the codebase.

The solution: A post-tool-use hook that runs tsc --no-emit after every file edit:

const { execSync } = require('child_process');

try {
  execSync('tsc --no-emit', { stdio: 'pipe' });
  process.exit(0);
} catch (error) {
  // Feed type errors back to Claude as feedback
  console.error('TypeScript errors:\n' + error.stdout.toString());
  process.exit(0); // post-hook can't block, but Claude reads the feedback
}
Claude edits a function signature
Hook runs tsc --no-emit
TypeScript compiler checks for errors without producing output files
Errors fed back to Claude
3 broken call sites found and sent via stderr
Claude auto-fixes all call sites
Reads the feedback and corrects each one
Adaptable

Works for any typed language with a type checker. For untyped languages, run your test suite instead of tsc.

Hook 2 — Duplicate Code Prevention

The problem: In complex multi-step tasks, Claude creates new queries/functions instead of reusing existing ones.

The solution: A post-tool hook launches a second Claude instance via the SDK to check for duplicates when a watched directory is edited.

Edit→A file in /queries/ is modified
Hook→Detects the change, launches secondary Claude via SDK
Check→Secondary Claude reads all existing queries and compares
Dupe!→exit(2) + "Use existing query X instead"
Clean→Primary Claude removes duplicate and reuses existing code
Trade-offs

✅ Cleaner codebase  |  ✅ Fewer bugs from duplicate logic  |  ❌ Slower  |  ❌ Higher token cost. Apply only to critical directories — not globally.

14

The Claude Code SDK

The Claude Code SDK lets you use Claude Code programmatically — in your own scripts, CI pipelines, and hooks — rather than just through the terminal.

Available for CLI, TypeScript / JavaScript, and Python.

Default Permissions

Safety First

By default, SDK instances only have read access (files, directories, grep). You must explicitly enable write operations via options.allowTools.

Basic Usage

import { query } from '@anthropic-ai/claude-code';

const result = await query({
  prompt: 'Refactor getUserById to be async',
  options: {
    allowTools: ['read', 'edit', 'grep'] // explicitly enable write
  }
});

// The final message is Claude's last response
const response = result.messages[result.messages.length - 1];

Best Suited For

Use CaseExample
Hooks (like duplicate detection)Second Claude instance reviewing changes
CI / CD pipelinesAutomated code quality review on every push
Custom helper scriptsPre-migration schema analysis
✓ Final Understanding Check
⚡

Quick Reference Cheat Sheet

Keyboard Shortcuts
Ctrl+VPaste screenshot into prompt
Shift+Tab ×2Enable Plan Mode
Escape ×1Stop Claude mid-response
Escape ×2Rewind conversation history
Slash Commands
/initAnalyze codebase, create Claude.md
/compactSummarize + clear clutter
/clearFull reset — fresh start
/hooksConfigure hooks interactively
Prompt Symbols
@fileInclude specific file in context
# instructionAdd permanent memory to Claude.md
Ultra thinkExtended reasoning mode
Hook Exit Codes
exit(0)Allow the tool call to proceed
exit(2)Block tool call (pre-hook only)
stderrFeedback message sent to Claude
Getting Started Path

1. Install Claude Code and run /init → 2. Try a simple bug fix task → 3. Add a Claude.md with your key files → 4. Create one custom command → 5. Explore MCP servers → 6. Add hooks for quality checks. The more context and structure you give Claude, the better it performs.

Built from Claude Code course notes — docs.anthropic.com

↑

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="UTF-8" />
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0" />
<title>Learn Claude Code — Interactive Study Guide</title>
<link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com" />
<link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Fraunces:opsz,wght@9..144,300;9..144,500;9..144,700;9..144,900&family=DM+Mono:wght@300;400;500&family=Source+Serif+4:ital,opsz,wght@0,8..60,300;0,8..60,400;0,8..60,600;1,8..60,400&display=swap" rel="stylesheet" />
<style>
:root {
  --bg: #faf6f0;
  --bg-warm: #f5efe5;
  --surface: #fff;
  --surface-alt: #fdf9f3;
  --ink: #1a1612;
  --ink-soft: #4a4035;
  --ink-muted: #8a7e70;
  --ink-faint: #c4b9a8;
  --accent: #c44d2b;
  --accent-light: #e86b4a;
  --accent-bg: #fef0ec;
  --teal: #1a7a6d;
  --teal-bg: #e8f5f2;
  --blue: #2d5fa6;
  --blue-bg: #eaf0fa;
  --purple: #6b4c9a;
  --purple-bg: #f2eef8;
  --gold: #a17c2e;
  --gold-bg: #faf3e0;
  --green: #3a7d44;
  --green-bg: #eaf5ec;
  --border: #e5ddd0;
  --border-light: #ede7dc;
  --shadow: 0 1px 3px rgba(26,22,18,0.06), 0 6px 16px rgba(26,22,18,0.04);
  --shadow-lg: 0 4px 12px rgba(26,22,18,0.08), 0 16px 40px rgba(26,22,18,0.06);
  --radius: 10px;
  --radius-sm: 6px;
}

*, *::before, *::after { box-sizing: border-box; margin: 0; padding: 0; }
html { scroll-behavior: smooth; scroll-padding-top: 80px; }

body {
  background: var(--bg);
  color: var(--ink);
  font-family: 'Source Serif 4', Georgia, serif;
  font-size: 17.5px;
  line-height: 1.78;
  -webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;
}

::selection { background: var(--accent-bg); color: var(--accent); }
::-webkit-scrollbar { width: 8px; }
::-webkit-scrollbar-track { background: var(--bg); }
::-webkit-scrollbar-thumb { background: var(--ink-faint); border-radius: 4px; }
::-webkit-scrollbar-thumb:hover { background: var(--ink-muted); }

/* ── NAV ── */
.topbar {
  position: fixed; top: 0; left: 0; right: 0; height: 60px;
  background: rgba(250,246,240,0.92); backdrop-filter: blur(12px);
  border-bottom: 1px solid var(--border-light);
  display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: space-between;
  padding: 0 clamp(1rem, 4vw, 3rem); z-index: 100;
}
.topbar-brand {
  font-family: 'Fraunces', serif; font-weight: 700; font-size: 1.05rem;
  color: var(--ink); display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 10px;
}
.topbar-brand .icon {
  width: 28px; height: 28px; background: var(--accent); border-radius: 6px;
  display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
  color: #fff; font-family: 'DM Mono', monospace; font-size: 0.75rem; font-weight: 500;
}
.topbar-nav { display: flex; gap: 0.25rem; list-style: none; }
.topbar-nav a {
  font-family: 'DM Mono', monospace; font-size: 0.7rem; font-weight: 400;
  color: var(--ink-muted); text-decoration: none; padding: 0.4rem 0.7rem;
  border-radius: var(--radius-sm); transition: all 0.2s;
  letter-spacing: 0.02em;
}
.topbar-nav a:hover { color: var(--accent); background: var(--accent-bg); }

.progress-track {
  position: fixed; top: 60px; left: 0; right: 0; height: 3px;
  background: var(--border-light); z-index: 99;
}
.progress-fill {
  height: 100%; width: 0%; background: linear-gradient(90deg, var(--accent), var(--accent-light));
  transition: width 0.15s; border-radius: 0 2px 2px 0;
}

/* ── LAYOUT ── */
.page { max-width: 780px; margin: 0 auto; padding: 0 clamp(1.25rem, 4vw, 2.5rem); }

/* ── HERO ── */
.hero {
  padding: 7rem 0 4rem; text-align: center; position: relative;
}
.hero-badge {
  display: inline-flex; align-items: center; gap: 6px;
  font-family: 'DM Mono', monospace; font-size: 0.72rem; font-weight: 500;
  color: var(--accent); background: var(--accent-bg);
  padding: 0.35rem 1rem; border-radius: 20px; margin-bottom: 1.5rem;
  letter-spacing: 0.04em;
}
.hero-badge .dot { width: 6px; height: 6px; background: var(--accent); border-radius: 50%; }
.hero h1 {
  font-family: 'Fraunces', serif; font-weight: 900;
  font-size: clamp(2.4rem, 6vw, 3.6rem); line-height: 1.1;
  letter-spacing: -0.025em; color: var(--ink); margin-bottom: 1rem;
}
.hero h1 em { font-style: normal; color: var(--accent); }
.hero-desc {
  font-size: 1.1rem; color: var(--ink-soft); max-width: 520px;
  margin: 0 auto 2rem; font-style: italic; line-height: 1.7;
}
.hero-meta {
  display: flex; justify-content: center; gap: 2.5rem; flex-wrap: wrap;
}
.hero-meta-item { text-align: center; }
.hero-meta-item .val {
  font-family: 'Fraunces', serif; font-weight: 700; font-size: 1.5rem;
  color: var(--accent); display: block;
}
.hero-meta-item .lbl {
  font-family: 'DM Mono', monospace; font-size: 0.65rem;
  color: var(--ink-muted); text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: 0.1em;
}

/* ── TABLE OF CONTENTS ── */
.toc {
  background: var(--surface); border: 1px solid var(--border);
  border-radius: var(--radius); padding: 2rem 2.25rem;
  margin: 2rem 0 3rem; box-shadow: var(--shadow);
}
.toc-title {
  font-family: 'DM Mono', monospace; font-size: 0.68rem; font-weight: 500;
  color: var(--accent); text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: 0.14em;
  margin-bottom: 1.25rem; display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 8px;
}
.toc-title::after { content: ''; flex: 1; height: 1px; background: var(--border); }
.toc-list {
  display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(310px, 1fr));
  gap: 0.15rem 2rem; list-style: none;
}
.toc-list a {
  display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 0.75rem;
  font-family: 'Source Serif 4', serif; font-size: 0.92rem;
  color: var(--ink-soft); text-decoration: none; padding: 0.4rem 0.5rem;
  border-radius: var(--radius-sm); transition: all 0.2s;
}
.toc-list a:hover { color: var(--accent); background: var(--accent-bg); }
.toc-num {
  font-family: 'DM Mono', monospace; font-size: 0.68rem; font-weight: 500;
  color: var(--ink-faint); min-width: 1.6rem; transition: color 0.2s;
}
.toc-list a:hover .toc-num { color: var(--accent); }

/* ── LESSON SECTIONS ── */
.lesson {
  padding: 3.5rem 0 2.5rem;
  border-bottom: 1px solid var(--border-light);
  opacity: 0; transform: translateY(20px);
  transition: opacity 0.5s ease, transform 0.5s ease;
}
.lesson.visible { opacity: 1; transform: translateY(0); }
.lesson:last-of-type { border-bottom: none; }

.lesson-head { display: flex; align-items: flex-start; gap: 1rem; margin-bottom: 1.75rem; }
.lesson-num {
  font-family: 'DM Mono', monospace; font-size: 0.65rem; font-weight: 500;
  color: var(--accent); background: var(--accent-bg);
  min-width: 2.4rem; height: 2.4rem; display: flex; align-items: center;
  justify-content: center; border-radius: 8px; flex-shrink: 0; margin-top: 2px;
  border: 1px solid rgba(196,77,43,0.15);
}
.lesson h2 {
  font-family: 'Fraunces', serif; font-weight: 700; font-size: 1.7rem;
  line-height: 1.2; letter-spacing: -0.015em; color: var(--ink);
}

.lesson h3 {
  font-family: 'Fraunces', serif; font-weight: 600; font-size: 1.12rem;
  color: var(--teal); margin: 2.25rem 0 0.75rem;
  padding-bottom: 0.4rem; border-bottom: 2px solid var(--teal-bg);
}

.lesson p { margin-bottom: 1rem; color: var(--ink-soft); }
.lesson strong { color: var(--ink); font-weight: 600; }

/* ── ANALOGY BOX ── */
.analogy {
  background: var(--purple-bg); border: 1px solid rgba(107,76,154,0.15);
  border-radius: var(--radius); padding: 1.4rem 1.6rem; margin: 1.5rem 0;
  position: relative;
}
.analogy::before {
  content: 'ANALOGY'; font-family: 'DM Mono', monospace;
  font-size: 0.6rem; font-weight: 500; letter-spacing: 0.15em;
  color: var(--purple); position: absolute; top: -0.5rem; left: 1.2rem;
  background: var(--purple-bg); padding: 0 0.5rem;
}
.analogy p { color: var(--ink-soft); font-style: italic; margin: 0; font-size: 0.95rem; }

/* ── CALLOUTS ── */
.callout {
  background: var(--surface); border: 1px solid var(--border);
  border-radius: var(--radius); padding: 1.25rem 1.5rem; margin: 1.5rem 0;
  border-left: 4px solid var(--teal);
}
.callout.warn { border-left-color: var(--accent); background: var(--accent-bg); }
.callout.tip { border-left-color: var(--green); background: var(--green-bg); }
.callout.info { border-left-color: var(--blue); background: var(--blue-bg); }
.callout.gold { border-left-color: var(--gold); background: var(--gold-bg); }
.callout-label {
  font-family: 'DM Mono', monospace; font-size: 0.62rem; font-weight: 500;
  letter-spacing: 0.13em; text-transform: uppercase; margin-bottom: 0.4rem;
  display: block; color: var(--teal);
}
.callout.warn .callout-label { color: var(--accent); }
.callout.tip .callout-label { color: var(--green); }
.callout.info .callout-label { color: var(--blue); }
.callout.gold .callout-label { color: var(--gold); }
.callout p { font-size: 0.93rem; color: var(--ink-soft); margin: 0; }

/* ── PROCESS STEPS ── */
.steps { margin: 1.5rem 0; }
.step {
  display: flex; gap: 1rem; position: relative;
  padding-bottom: 1.25rem;
}
.step:last-child { padding-bottom: 0; }
.step-rail {
  display: flex; flex-direction: column; align-items: center; width: 1.8rem; flex-shrink: 0;
}
.step-dot {
  width: 10px; height: 10px; border-radius: 50%; border: 2.5px solid var(--accent);
  background: var(--bg); flex-shrink: 0; margin-top: 0.55rem; position: relative; z-index: 1;
}
.step-line { width: 2px; flex: 1; background: var(--border); margin-top: 4px; }
.step:last-child .step-line { display: none; }
.step-body { flex: 1; padding-top: 0.15rem; }
.step-title {
  font-family: 'DM Mono', monospace; font-size: 0.75rem; font-weight: 500;
  color: var(--accent); text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: 0.06em;
  margin-bottom: 0.15rem;
}
.step-text { font-size: 0.92rem; color: var(--ink-soft); line-height: 1.6; }

/* ── CODE BLOCKS ── */
pre {
  background: #1e1b18; color: #e0d8cc; border-radius: var(--radius);
  padding: 1.3rem 1.5rem; margin: 1.25rem 0; overflow-x: auto;
  position: relative; box-shadow: var(--shadow);
  border: 1px solid rgba(0,0,0,0.1);
}
pre::before {
  content: attr(data-lang); position: absolute; top: 0; right: 0;
  font-family: 'DM Mono', monospace; font-size: 0.58rem; letter-spacing: 0.1em;
  text-transform: uppercase; color: #8a7e6a; padding: 0.3rem 0.8rem;
  background: rgba(255,255,255,0.04); border-radius: 0 var(--radius) 0 var(--radius-sm);
}
code {
  font-family: 'DM Mono', monospace; font-size: 0.84rem; line-height: 1.7;
}
p code, li code, td code {
  background: var(--gold-bg); border: 1px solid rgba(161,124,46,0.2);
  color: var(--gold); padding: 0.12em 0.4em; font-size: 0.82rem;
  border-radius: 4px;
}
.ck { color: #f0836a; } .cs { color: #d4c47a; } .cc { color: #6b6358; font-style: italic; }
.cn { color: #7cc5bd; } .cf { color: #8dc77b; } .cv { color: #e0b060; }

/* ── TABLES ── */
.table-wrap { overflow-x: auto; margin: 1.5rem 0; border-radius: var(--radius); box-shadow: var(--shadow); }
table { width: 100%; border-collapse: collapse; background: var(--surface); font-size: 0.92rem; }
thead th {
  font-family: 'DM Mono', monospace; font-size: 0.68rem; font-weight: 500;
  letter-spacing: 0.08em; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--accent);
  padding: 0.8rem 1.1rem; text-align: left; background: var(--accent-bg);
  border-bottom: 2px solid rgba(196,77,43,0.15);
}
tbody td {
  padding: 0.75rem 1.1rem; border-bottom: 1px solid var(--border-light);
  color: var(--ink-soft); vertical-align: top;
}
tbody tr:last-child td { border-bottom: none; }
tbody tr:hover td { background: rgba(196,77,43,0.02); }
.badge {
  display: inline-block; font-family: 'DM Mono', monospace; font-size: 0.68rem;
  padding: 0.15em 0.6em; border-radius: 4px; font-weight: 500;
}
.badge-yes { background: var(--green-bg); color: var(--green); }
.badge-no { background: var(--accent-bg); color: var(--accent); }

/* ── SHORTCUT GRID ── */
.card-grid {
  display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(200px, 1fr));
  gap: 0.85rem; margin: 1.5rem 0;

content.content:
600→

Pasting Screenshots — Ctrl+V

601→
602→ ⚠ Mac Gotcha 603→

Use Control+V, not Command+V. Command+V does not paste screenshots into Claude Code on macOS.

604→
605→

Screenshot a broken UI element, paste it, and say "fix the alignment here." No need to describe what you see in words.

606→ 607→

Plan Mode — For Breadth

608→

Activate with Shift + Tab (twice). Claude will research all relevant files, build a detailed plan, and only then start making changes. Ideal for multi-file features and refactoring.

609→ 610→

Thinking Mode — For Depth

611→

Include phrases like "Ultra think" in your message. Gives Claude an extended reasoning budget for complex logic and subtle bugs.

612→ 
Ultra think — why does our payment processing occasionally fail
   613→for orders placed exactly at midnight?
614→ 615→

When to Use Each Mode

616→
617→ 618→ 619→ 620→ 621→ 622→ 623→
ModeHandlesBest For
Plan ModeBreadthMulti-step tasks, wide codebase understanding
Thinking ModeDepthTricky logic, debugging specific issues
Both combinedBreadth + DepthThe most complex tasks you face
624→ 625→
626→ Cost Note 627→

Both modes consume additional tokens. Use them strategically rather than on every request.

628→
629→ 630→

Git Integration

631→

Claude Code can stage files, create commits, and write descriptive commit messages automatically. Just say "commit the changes you just made."

632→ 633→ 634→ 635→
636→
07

Controlling Context

637→

Long conversations accumulate noise that can confuse Claude. These tools keep you focused:

638→ 639→
640→
Escape ×1
Stop Claude mid-response and redirect
641→
Escape + #
Stop, then add permanent memory to prevent repeated mistakes
642→
Escape ×2
Rewind: jump back to any earlier message
643→
/compact
Summarize conversation, preserve knowledge, clear clutter
644→
/clear
Wipe entire conversation — fresh start for a new task
645→
646→ 647→
648→ Pro Tip 649→

Use Double Escape when you've gone down a debugging rabbit hole for 10+ messages with no results. Jump back to before the rabbit hole and try a different approach — without losing relevant context.

650→
651→
652→ 653→ 654→
655→
08

Custom Commands

656→

Custom commands let you create your own slash commands to automate repetitive tasks.

657→ 658→

How to Create One

659→
660→
Create folder
.claude/commands/ in your project root
661→
Create a markdown file
Filename becomes the command name: audit.md → /audit
662→
Write instructions
Plain English instructions Claude will follow when you invoke the command
663→
Restart Claude Code
Required to activate new command files
664→
665→ 666→

Example — /audit Command

667→ 
Audit all npm dependencies in package.json for known vulnerabilities.
   668→For each vulnerable package:
   669→1. List the package name and current version
   670→2. Describe the vulnerability
   671→3. Suggest the safest upgrade path
672→ 673→

Commands with Arguments

674→ 
Generate comprehensive unit tests for the file at: $arguments
   675→
   676→Include tests for:
   677→- Happy path
   678→- Edge cases
   679→- Error handling
680→ 
/test src/components/Button.tsx
681→
682→ 683→ 684→
685→
09

MCP Servers — Extending Claude's Abilities

686→

MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers are external tools you plug into Claude Code to give it new capabilities. Think of them as "apps" Claude can use — browsers, databases, design tools, and more.

687→ 688→

Installing an MCP Server

689→ 
# Install the Playwright browser automation server
   690→claude mcp add playwright npx @playwright/mcp
691→ 692→

Auto-Approving MCP Tools

693→ 
{
   694→  "allow": ["MCP__playwright"]
   695→}
696→ 697→

Real World — Automated UI Refinement

698→
699→
1→Opens your app at localhost:3000 in a real browser
700→
2→Takes a screenshot, analyzes UI quality
701→
3→Edits the component code to improve styling
702→
4→Takes another screenshot, compares to the first
703→
5→Iterates automatically until satisfied — no human needed
704→
705→
706→ 707→ 708→
709→
10

GitHub Integration

710→

Claude Code can run directly inside GitHub Actions, triggered by pull requests and issues.

711→ 712→

Setup

713→ 
/install-github-app
714→

This auto-creates two GitHub Actions in your repo:

715→ 716→
717→ 718→ 719→ 720→ 721→ 722→
ActionWhat It Does
Mention supportTag @claude in any issue or PR comment to assign Claude a task
PR reviewClaude automatically reviews every new pull request
723→ 724→
725→ Important: Permissions 726→

You must explicitly list all permissions for Claude Code in your Actions config. MCP server tools require individual permission listing — there are no shortcuts.

727→
728→
729→ 730→ 731→
732→
11

Hooks — Automating Quality Control

733→

Hooks are custom scripts that run automatically before or after Claude uses a tool. They let you enforce rules, run checks, and provide automated feedback.

734→ 735→

The Two Hook Types

736→
737→ 738→ 739→ 740→ 741→ 742→
TypeWhen It RunsCan Block?
Pre-tool useBefore Claude executes a tool✓ Yes (exit 2)
Post-tool useAfter Claude executes a tool✗ No
743→ 744→

How a Hook Decision Works

745→
746→
Claude→Wants to read a file
747→
Hook→Your pre-tool hook script runs first
748→
Check→Is this file .env?
749→
YES→exit(2) — Claude is blocked + gets your error message via stderr
750→
NO→exit(0) — Claude proceeds normally
751→
752→ 753→

Configuration

754→ 
{
   755→  "hooks": {
   756→    "preToolUse": [
   757→      {
   758→        "matcher": "read|grep",
   759→        "command": "node /absolute/path/to/hooks/read_hook.js"
   760→      }
   761→    ]
   762→  }
   763→}
764→ 765→

Hook Script Example

766→ 
const chunks = [];
   767→
   768→process.stdin.on('data', chunk => chunks.push(chunk));
   769→process.stdin.on('end', () => {
   770→  const data = JSON.parse(Buffer.concat(chunks).toString());
   771→  const filePath = data.tool_input?.path || '';
   772→
   773→  if (filePath.includes('.env')) {
   774→    console.error('Blocked: .env files are off-limits for security');
   775→    process.exit(2); // Block the tool call
   776→  }
   777→
   778→  process.exit(0); // Allow the tool call
   779→});
780→ 781→
782→ How Data Flows 783→

The hook receives a JSON object via stdin containing: session_id, tool_name (e.g., "read" or "grep"), and tool_input (including the file path). Your script parses this and exits with the appropriate code.

784→
785→
786→ 787→ 788→
789→
12

Gotchas Around Hooks

790→ 791→

The Absolute Path Problem

792→

Claude Code recommends absolute paths in hook commands for security (prevents path interception attacks). But absolute paths are machine-specific, making it hard to share settings with your team.

793→ 794→
795→ The Problem 796→

Your machine: /Users/alice/myproject/hooks/read_hook.js
Colleague's machine: /Users/bob/work/myproject/hooks/read_hook.js
Committing your path breaks it for everyone else.

797→
798→ 799→

The Solution — Template + Setup Script

800→

Create a settings.example.json template with a $PWD placeholder:

801→

file.content:
Pasting Screenshots — Ctrl+V

⚠ Mac Gotcha

Use Control+V, not Command+V. Command+V does not paste screenshots into Claude Code on macOS.

Screenshot a broken UI element, paste it, and say "fix the alignment here." No need to describe what you see in words.

Plan Mode — For Breadth

Activate with Shift + Tab (twice). Claude will research all relevant files, build a detailed plan, and only then start making changes. Ideal for multi-file features and refactoring.

Thinking Mode — For Depth

Include phrases like "Ultra think" in your message. Gives Claude an extended reasoning budget for complex logic and subtle bugs.

Ultra think — why does our payment processing occasionally fail
for orders placed exactly at midnight?

When to Use Each Mode

ModeHandlesBest For
Plan ModeBreadthMulti-step tasks, wide codebase understanding
Thinking ModeDepthTricky logic, debugging specific issues
Both combinedBreadth + DepthThe most complex tasks you face
Cost Note

Both modes consume additional tokens. Use them strategically rather than on every request.

Git Integration

Claude Code can stage files, create commits, and write descriptive commit messages automatically. Just say "commit the changes you just made."

07

Controlling Context

Long conversations accumulate noise that can confuse Claude. These tools keep you focused:

Escape ×1
Stop Claude mid-response and redirect
Escape + #
Stop, then add permanent memory to prevent repeated mistakes
Escape ×2
Rewind: jump back to any earlier message
/compact
Summarize conversation, preserve knowledge, clear clutter
/clear
Wipe entire conversation — fresh start for a new task
Pro Tip

Use Double Escape when you've gone down a debugging rabbit hole for 10+ messages with no results. Jump back to before the rabbit hole and try a different approach — without losing relevant context.

08

Custom Commands

Custom commands let you create your own slash commands to automate repetitive tasks.

How to Create One

Create folder
.claude/commands/ in your project root
Create a markdown file
Filename becomes the command name: audit.md → /audit
Write instructions
Plain English instructions Claude will follow when you invoke the command
Restart Claude Code
Required to activate new command files

Example — /audit Command

Audit all npm dependencies in package.json for known vulnerabilities.
For each vulnerable package:
1. List the package name and current version
2. Describe the vulnerability
3. Suggest the safest upgrade path

Commands with Arguments

Generate comprehensive unit tests for the file at: $arguments

Include tests for:
- Happy path
- Edge cases
- Error handling
/test src/components/Button.tsx
09

MCP Servers — Extending Claude's Abilities

MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers are external tools you plug into Claude Code to give it new capabilities. Think of them as "apps" Claude can use — browsers, databases, design tools, and more.

Installing an MCP Server

# Install the Playwright browser automation server
claude mcp add playwright npx @playwright/mcp

Auto-Approving MCP Tools

{
  "allow": ["MCP__playwright"]
}

Real World — Automated UI Refinement

1→Opens your app at localhost:3000 in a real browser
2→Takes a screenshot, analyzes UI quality
3→Edits the component code to improve styling
4→Takes another screenshot, compares to the first
5→Iterates automatically until satisfied — no human needed
10

GitHub Integration

Claude Code can run directly inside GitHub Actions, triggered by pull requests and issues.

Setup

/install-github-app

This auto-creates two GitHub Actions in your repo:

ActionWhat It Does
Mention supportTag @claude in any issue or PR comment to assign Claude a task
PR reviewClaude automatically reviews every new pull request
Important: Permissions

You must explicitly list all permissions for Claude Code in your Actions config. MCP server tools require individual permission listing — there are no shortcuts.

11

Hooks — Automating Quality Control

Hooks are custom scripts that run automatically before or after Claude uses a tool. They let you enforce rules, run checks, and provide automated feedback.

The Two Hook Types

TypeWhen It RunsCan Block?
Pre-tool useBefore Claude executes a tool✓ Yes (exit 2)
Post-tool useAfter Claude executes a tool✗ No

How a Hook Decision Works

Claude→Wants to read a file
Hook→Your pre-tool hook script runs first
Check→Is this file .env?
YES→exit(2) — Claude is blocked + gets your error message via stderr
NO→exit(0) — Claude proceeds normally

Configuration

{
  "hooks": {
    "preToolUse": [
      {
        "matcher": "read|grep",
        "command": "node /absolute/path/to/hooks/read_hook.js"
      }
    ]
  }
}

Hook Script Example

const chunks = [];

process.stdin.on('data', chunk => chunks.push(chunk));
process.stdin.on('end', () => {
  const data = JSON.parse(Buffer.concat(chunks).toString());
  const filePath = data.tool_input?.path || '';

  if (filePath.includes('.env')) {
    console.error('Blocked: .env files are off-limits for security');
    process.exit(2); // Block the tool call
  }

  process.exit(0); // Allow the tool call
});
How Data Flows

The hook receives a JSON object via stdin containing: session_id, tool_name (e.g., "read" or "grep"), and tool_input (including the file path). Your script parses this and exits with the appropriate code.

12

Gotchas Around Hooks

The Absolute Path Problem

Claude Code recommends absolute paths in hook commands for security (prevents path interception attacks). But absolute paths are machine-specific, making it hard to share settings with your team.

The Problem

Your machine: /Users/alice/myproject/hooks/read_hook.js
Colleague's machine: /Users/bob/work/myproject/hooks/read_hook.js
Committing your path breaks it for everyone else.

The Solution — Template + Setup Script

Create a settings.example.json template with a $PWD placeholder:

{
  "hooks": {
    "preToolUse": [{
      "matcher": "read|grep",
      "command": "node $PWD/hooks/read_hook.js"
    }]
  }
}

A scripts/init-claude.js setup script replaces $PWD with each developer's actual project path when they run:

npm run setup   # installs deps + generates settings.local.json
Result

Commit settings.example.json to git. Add settings.local.json to .gitignore. Every developer gets correct absolute paths automatically.

Remember

You must restart Claude Code after any hook configuration changes for them to take effect.

13

Useful Hooks — Real Examples

Hook 1 — TypeScript Type Checker

The problem: Claude edits a function's signature but forgets to update all call sites, leaving type errors scattered in the codebase.

The solution: A post-tool-use hook that runs tsc --no-emit after every file edit:

const { execSync } = require('child_process');

try {
  execSync('tsc --no-emit', { stdio: 'pipe' });
  process.exit(0);
} catch (error) {
  // Feed type errors back to Claude as feedback
  console.error('TypeScript errors:\n' + error.stdout.toString());
  process.exit(0); // post-hook can't block, but Claude reads the feedback
}
Claude edits a function signature
Hook runs tsc --no-emit
TypeScript compiler checks for errors without producing output files
Errors fed back to Claude
3 broken call sites found and sent via stderr
Claude auto-fixes all call sites
Reads the feedback and corrects each one
Adaptable

Works for any typed language with a type checker. For untyped languages, run your test suite instead of tsc.

Hook 2 — Duplicate Code Prevention

The problem: In complex multi-step tasks, Claude creates new queries/functions instead of reusing existing ones.

The solution: A post-tool hook launches a second Claude instance via the SDK to check for duplicates when a watched directory is edited.

Edit→A file in /queries/ is modified
Hook→Detects the change, launches secondary Claude via SDK
Check→Secondary Claude reads all existing queries and compares
Dupe!→exit(2) + "Use existing query X instead"
Clean→Primary Claude removes duplicate and reuses existing code
Trade-offs

✅ Cleaner codebase  |  ✅ Fewer bugs from duplicate logic  |  ❌ Slower  |  ❌ Higher token cost. Apply only to critical directories — not globally.

14

The Claude Code SDK

The Claude Code SDK lets you use Claude Code programmatically — in your own scripts, CI pipelines, and hooks — rather than just through the terminal.

Available for CLI, TypeScript / JavaScript, and Python.

Default Permissions

Safety First

By default, SDK instances only have read access (files, directories, grep). You must explicitly enable write operations via options.allowTools.

Basic Usage

import { query } from '@anthropic-ai/claude-code';

const result = await query({
  prompt: 'Refactor getUserById to be async',
  options: {
    allowTools: ['read', 'edit', 'grep'] // explicitly enable write
  }
});

// The final message is Claude's last response
const response = result.messages[result.messages.length - 1];

Best Suited For

Use CaseExample
Hooks (like duplicate detection)Second Claude instance reviewing changes
CI / CD pipelinesAutomated code quality review on every push
Custom helper scriptsPre-migration schema analysis
✓ Final Understanding Check
⚡

Quick Reference Cheat Sheet

Keyboard Shortcuts
Ctrl+VPaste screenshot into prompt
Shift+Tab ×2Enable Plan Mode
Escape ×1Stop Claude mid-response
Escape ×2Rewind conversation history
Slash Commands
/initAnalyze codebase, create Claude.md
/compactSummarize + clear clutter
/clearFull reset — fresh start
/hooksConfigure hooks interactively
Prompt Symbols
@fileInclude specific file in context
# instructionAdd permanent memory to Claude.md
Ultra thinkExtended reasoning mode
Hook Exit Codes
exit(0)Allow the tool call to proceed
exit(2)Block tool call (pre-hook only)
stderrFeedback message sent to Claude
Getting Started Path

1. Install Claude Code and run /init → 2. Try a simple bug fix task → 3. Add a Claude.md with your key files → 4. Create one custom command → 5. Explore MCP servers → 6. Add hooks for quality checks. The more context and structure you give Claude, the better it performs.

CC
Learn Claude Code
  • Foundations
  • Context
  • Commands
  • Hooks
  • SDK
  • Cheatsheet
Interactive Study Guide

Master Claude Code
from Scratch

Everything you need to know — from what a coding assistant is, to custom hooks and the SDK. Verified, organized, and built for focused learning.

14Lessons
3.9×Real perf gain
∞Extensibility
Curriculum
  • 01 What is a Coding Assistant?
  • 02 What is Claude Code?
  • 03 How Tool Use Works
  • 04 Claude Code in Action
  • 05 Adding Context
  • 06 Making Changes & Power Modes
  • 07 Controlling Context
  • 08 Custom Commands
  • 09 MCP Servers
  • 10 GitHub Integration
  • 11 Hooks — Automating Quality
  • 12 Gotchas Around Hooks
  • 13 Useful Hooks — Real Examples
  • 14 The Claude Code SDK
01

What is a Coding Assistant?

A coding assistant is a tool that sits between you and a language model (like Claude), helping you write, fix, and manage code. It can read your files, run your tests, and make changes — all from a single conversation.

The Core Workflow

You send a task
"Fix the login bug described in this error message"
Assistant wraps the request
The coding assistant layer adds tool instructions to your message
Model gathers context
Reads relevant files, understands the codebase structure
Plan is formulated
A step-by-step approach is created before any changes
Action is taken
Files are edited, tests run, and results returned to you

Imagine hiring a freelance developer. You email them: "our checkout page is broken." A good developer doesn't guess — they look at the code, reproduce the issue, trace the logic, then fix it. A coding assistant works the same way, but faster.

Key Limitation

Language models can only process text input and produce text output. They cannot directly read files, run commands, or interact with external systems. This is why tool use is essential.

02

What is Claude Code?

Claude Code is Anthropic's official coding assistant built on the Claude language model. You interact with it through the terminal, and it can read files, write code, run commands, and connect to external services.

Why Claude Code?

FeatureWhat It Means for You
Superior tool useExceptionally good at knowing which tool to use and when — better than competing models
Extensible by designPlug in new tools (browsers, databases, APIs) as your needs grow via MCP servers
Better securitySearches your code directly — doesn't send your entire codebase to external indexing servers
Complex task handlingChains multiple tools together to solve multi-step problems effectively
03

How Tool Use Works

This is the most important concept to internalize. Language models can only process text. They cannot open a file, run a command, or check a website on their own. Tool use is the mechanism that bridges this gap.

Step-by-Step Breakdown

// 1. You ask:
"What's in my package.json?"

// 2. Claude Code adds instructions to the request:
"If you need to read a file, respond with: READ_FILE: <filename>"

// 3. The language model responds:
READ_FILE: package.json

// 4. Claude Code (assistant layer) actually reads the file

// 5. File contents are sent back to the language model

// 6. Claude reads the content and gives you a real answer

Think of a surgeon and a scrub nurse. The surgeon (language model) says "scalpel" and the nurse (Claude Code) physically hands it over. The surgeon has the knowledge; the nurse does the physical work. Together they complete the operation.

Remember

All language models require tool use for non-text tasks. The quality of tool use directly impacts how effective a coding assistant is. Claude's strength here is what makes Claude Code powerful.

04

Claude Code in Action

Example 1 — Performance Optimization

Claude analyzed the Chalk JavaScript library (~430M weekly downloads) and achieved a 3.9× throughput improvement:

Benchmark
Runs baseline performance benchmarks to measure current speed
Profile
Uses profiling tools to find the slowest code paths
Plan
Creates a prioritized to-do list of bottlenecks to fix
Fix & Verify
Implements changes and re-runs benchmarks to confirm improvement

Example 2 — PII Security Detection

Auto-detected in PR Review

A developer adds user email to a Lambda function output. Claude Code, running on GitHub Actions, reads the Terraform infrastructure, traces data flow, detects that emails now reach an external S3 bucket shared with a partner, and posts a warning before merge.

Example 3 — Data Analysis

Point Claude at a CSV of customer data. It opens a Jupyter notebook, writes and runs cells iteratively, identifies churn patterns, and produces charts — all in one conversation.

✓ Check Your Understanding
05

Adding Context

Claude works best when it understands your project. Too little context gives wrong answers. Too much irrelevant context makes it slower and less accurate. The goal is just enough.

The /init Command

/init

Scans your entire codebase, understands the architecture, and creates a Claude.md file. This file is automatically included in every future request.

Three Types of Claude.md

TypeCommitted?Use Case
Project level✓ YesArchitecture, coding standards, key files — shared with team
Local level✗ NoPersonal preferences, notes just for you
Machine level✗ NoGlobal preferences across all your projects

Example Claude.md

## Project: E-commerce Platform
- Database schema → /db/schema.sql (always reference for DB questions)
- We use PostgreSQL, not MySQL
- Auth handled by /src/auth/middleware.ts
- Never modify files in /vendor/

The @ Symbol — Pinpoint Context

@src/auth/middleware.ts — why is the login token expiring early?

Mention files directly instead of letting Claude search. It's like handing a new employee the exact page they need instead of saying "the answer is somewhere in the wiki."

The # Symbol — Memory Mode

# Always use async/await instead of .then() chains

Immediately updates your Claude.md so Claude remembers this in all future interactions.

06

Making Changes & Power Modes

Pasting Screenshots — Ctrl+V

⚠ Mac Gotcha

Use Control+V, not Command+V. Command+V does not paste screenshots into Claude Code on macOS.

Screenshot a broken UI element, paste it, and say "fix the alignment here." No need to describe what you see in words.

Plan Mode — For Breadth

Activate with Shift + Tab (twice). Claude will research all relevant files, build a detailed plan, and only then start making changes. Ideal for multi-file features and refactoring.

Thinking Mode — For Depth

Include phrases like "Ultra think" in your message. Gives Claude an extended reasoning budget for complex logic and subtle bugs.

Ultra think — why does our payment processing occasionally fail
for orders placed exactly at midnight?

When to Use Each Mode

ModeHandlesBest For
Plan ModeBreadthMulti-step tasks, wide codebase understanding
Thinking ModeDepthTricky logic, debugging specific issues
Both combinedBreadth + DepthThe most complex tasks you face
Cost Note

Both modes consume additional tokens. Use them strategically rather than on every request.

Git Integration

Claude Code can stage files, create commits, and write descriptive commit messages automatically. Just say "commit the changes you just made."

07

Controlling Context

Long conversations accumulate noise that can confuse Claude. These tools keep you focused:

Escape ×1
Stop Claude mid-response and redirect
Escape + #
Stop, then add permanent memory to prevent repeated mistakes
Escape ×2
Rewind: jump back to any earlier message
/compact
Summarize conversation, preserve knowledge, clear clutter
/clear
Wipe entire conversation — fresh start for a new task
Pro Tip

Use Double Escape when you've gone down a debugging rabbit hole for 10+ messages with no results. Jump back to before the rabbit hole and try a different approach — without losing relevant context.

08

Custom Commands

Custom commands let you create your own slash commands to automate repetitive tasks.

How to Create One

Create folder
.claude/commands/ in your project root
Create a markdown file
Filename becomes the command name: audit.md → /audit
Write instructions
Plain English instructions Claude will follow when you invoke the command
Restart Claude Code
Required to activate new command files

Example — /audit Command

Audit all npm dependencies in package.json for known vulnerabilities.
For each vulnerable package:
1. List the package name and current version
2. Describe the vulnerability
3. Suggest the safest upgrade path

Commands with Arguments

Generate comprehensive unit tests for the file at: $arguments

Include tests for:
- Happy path
- Edge cases
- Error handling
/test src/components/Button.tsx
09

MCP Servers — Extending Claude's Abilities

MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers are external tools you plug into Claude Code to give it new capabilities. Think of them as "apps" Claude can use — browsers, databases, design tools, and more.

Installing an MCP Server

# Install the Playwright browser automation server
claude mcp add playwright npx @playwright/mcp

Auto-Approving MCP Tools

{
  "allow": ["MCP__playwright"]
}

Real World — Automated UI Refinement

1→Opens your app at localhost:3000 in a real browser
2→Takes a screenshot, analyzes UI quality
3→Edits the component code to improve styling
4→Takes another screenshot, compares to the first
5→Iterates automatically until satisfied — no human needed
10

GitHub Integration

Claude Code can run directly inside GitHub Actions, triggered by pull requests and issues.

Setup

/install-github-app

This auto-creates two GitHub Actions in your repo:

ActionWhat It Does
Mention supportTag @claude in any issue or PR comment to assign Claude a task
PR reviewClaude automatically reviews every new pull request
Important: Permissions

You must explicitly list all permissions for Claude Code in your Actions config. MCP server tools require individual permission listing — there are no shortcuts.

11

Hooks — Automating Quality Control

Hooks are custom scripts that run automatically before or after Claude uses a tool. They let you enforce rules, run checks, and provide automated feedback.

The Two Hook Types

TypeWhen It RunsCan Block?
Pre-tool useBefore Claude executes a tool✓ Yes (exit 2)
Post-tool useAfter Claude executes a tool✗ No

How a Hook Decision Works

Claude→Wants to read a file
Hook→Your pre-tool hook script runs first
Check→Is this file .env?
YES→exit(2) — Claude is blocked + gets your error message via stderr
NO→exit(0) — Claude proceeds normally

Configuration

{
  "hooks": {
    "preToolUse": [
      {
        "matcher": "read|grep",
        "command": "node /absolute/path/to/hooks/read_hook.js"
      }
    ]
  }
}

Hook Script Example

const chunks = [];

process.stdin.on('data', chunk => chunks.push(chunk));
process.stdin.on('end', () => {
  const data = JSON.parse(Buffer.concat(chunks).toString());
  const filePath = data.tool_input?.path || '';

  if (filePath.includes('.env')) {
    console.error('Blocked: .env files are off-limits for security');
    process.exit(2); // Block the tool call
  }

  process.exit(0); // Allow the tool call
});
How Data Flows

The hook receives a JSON object via stdin containing: session_id, tool_name (e.g., "read" or "grep"), and tool_input (including the file path). Your script parses this and exits with the appropriate code.

12

Gotchas Around Hooks

The Absolute Path Problem

Claude Code recommends absolute paths in hook commands for security (prevents path interception attacks). But absolute paths are machine-specific, making it hard to share settings with your team.

The Problem

Your machine: /Users/alice/myproject/hooks/read_hook.js
Colleague's machine: /Users/bob/work/myproject/hooks/read_hook.js
Committing your path breaks it for everyone else.

The Solution — Template + Setup Script

Create a settings.example.json template with a $PWD placeholder:

{
  "hooks": {
    "preToolUse": [{
      "matcher": "read|grep",
      "command": "node $PWD/hooks/read_hook.js"
    }]
  }
}

A scripts/init-claude.js setup script replaces $PWD with each developer's actual project path when they run:

npm run setup   # installs deps + generates settings.local.json
Result

Commit settings.example.json to git. Add settings.local.json to .gitignore. Every developer gets correct absolute paths automatically.

Remember

You must restart Claude Code after any hook configuration changes for them to take effect.

13

Useful Hooks — Real Examples

Hook 1 — TypeScript Type Checker

The problem: Claude edits a function's signature but forgets to update all call sites, leaving type errors scattered in the codebase.

The solution: A post-tool-use hook that runs tsc --no-emit after every file edit:

const { execSync } = require('child_process');

try {
  execSync('tsc --no-emit', { stdio: 'pipe' });
  process.exit(0);
} catch (error) {
  // Feed type errors back to Claude as feedback
  console.error('TypeScript errors:\n' + error.stdout.toString());
  process.exit(0); // post-hook can't block, but Claude reads the feedback
}
Claude edits a function signature
Hook runs tsc --no-emit
TypeScript compiler checks for errors without producing output files
Errors fed back to Claude
3 broken call sites found and sent via stderr
Claude auto-fixes all call sites
Reads the feedback and corrects each one
Adaptable

Works for any typed language with a type checker. For untyped languages, run your test suite instead of tsc.

Hook 2 — Duplicate Code Prevention

The problem: In complex multi-step tasks, Claude creates new queries/functions instead of reusing existing ones.

The solution: A post-tool hook launches a second Claude instance via the SDK to check for duplicates when a watched directory is edited.

Edit→A file in /queries/ is modified
Hook→Detects the change, launches secondary Claude via SDK
Check→Secondary Claude reads all existing queries and compares
Dupe!→exit(2) + "Use existing query X instead"
Clean→Primary Claude removes duplicate and reuses existing code
Trade-offs

✅ Cleaner codebase  |  ✅ Fewer bugs from duplicate logic  |  ❌ Slower  |  ❌ Higher token cost. Apply only to critical directories — not globally.

14

The Claude Code SDK

The Claude Code SDK lets you use Claude Code programmatically — in your own scripts, CI pipelines, and hooks — rather than just through the terminal.

Available for CLI, TypeScript / JavaScript, and Python.

Default Permissions

Safety First

By default, SDK instances only have read access (files, directories, grep). You must explicitly enable write operations via options.allowTools.

Basic Usage

import { query } from '@anthropic-ai/claude-code';

const result = await query({
  prompt: 'Refactor getUserById to be async',
  options: {
    allowTools: ['read', 'edit', 'grep'] // explicitly enable write
  }
});

// The final message is Claude's last response
const response = result.messages[result.messages.length - 1];

Best Suited For

Use CaseExample
Hooks (like duplicate detection)Second Claude instance reviewing changes
CI / CD pipelinesAutomated code quality review on every push
Custom helper scriptsPre-migration schema analysis
✓ Final Understanding Check
⚡

Ctrl+VPaste screenshot into prompt
Shift+Tab ×2Enable Plan Mode
Escape ×1Stop Claude mid-response
Escape ×2Rewind conversation history
Slash Commands
/initAnalyze codebase, create Claude.md

/hooksConfigure hooks interactively
Prompt Symbols

# instructionAdd permanent memory to Claude.md
Ultra thinkExtended reasoning mode
Hook Exit Codes
exit(0)Allow the tool call to proceed
exit(2)Block tool call (pre-hook only)
stderrFeedback message sent to Claude
Getting Started Path

1. Install Claude Code and run /init → 2. Try a simple bug fix task → 3. Add a Claude.md with your key files → 4. Create one custom command → 5. Explore MCP servers → 6. Add hooks for quality checks. The more context and structure you give Claude, the better it performs.

01

What is a Coding Assistant?

A coding assistant is a tool that sits between you and a language model (like Claude), helping you write, fix, and manage code. It can read your files, run your tests, and make changes — all from a single conversation.

The Core Workflow

You send a task
"Fix the login bug described in this error message"
Assistant wraps the request
The coding assistant layer adds tool instructions to your message
Model gathers context
Reads relevant files, understands the codebase structure
Plan is formulated
A step-by-step approach is created before any changes
Action is taken
Files are edited, tests run, and results returned to you

Imagine hiring a freelance developer. You email them: "our checkout page is broken." A good developer doesn't guess — they look at the code, reproduce the issue, trace the logic, then fix it. A coding assistant works the same way, but faster.

Key Limitation

Language models can only process text input and produce text output. They cannot directly read files, run commands, or interact with external systems. This is why tool use is essential.

02

What is Claude Code?

Claude Code is Anthropic's official coding assistant built on the Claude language model. You interact with it through the terminal, and it can read files, write code, run commands, and connect to external services.

Why Claude Code?

FeatureWhat It Means for You
Superior tool useExceptionally good at knowing which tool to use and when — better than competing models
Extensible by designPlug in new tools (browsers, databases, APIs) as your needs grow via MCP servers
Better securitySearches your code directly — doesn't send your entire codebase to external indexing servers
Complex task handlingChains multiple tools together to solve multi-step problems effectively
03

How Tool Use Works

This is the most important concept to internalize. Language models can only process text. They cannot open a file, run a command, or check a website on their own. Tool use is the mechanism that bridges this gap.

Step-by-Step Breakdown

// 1. You ask:
"What's in my package.json?"

// 2. Claude Code adds instructions to the request:
"If you need to read a file, respond with: READ_FILE: <filename>"

// 3. The language model responds:
READ_FILE: package.json

// 4. Claude Code (assistant layer) actually reads the file

// 5. File contents are sent back to the language model

// 6. Claude reads the content and gives you a real answer

Think of a surgeon and a scrub nurse. The surgeon (language model) says "scalpel" and the nurse (Claude Code) physically hands it over. The surgeon has the knowledge; the nurse does the physical work. Together they complete the operation.

Remember

All language models require tool use for non-text tasks. The quality of tool use directly impacts how effective a coding assistant is. Claude's strength here is what makes Claude Code powerful.

04

Claude Code in Action

Example 1 — Performance Optimization

Claude analyzed the Chalk JavaScript library (~430M weekly downloads) and achieved a 3.9× throughput improvement:

Benchmark
Runs baseline performance benchmarks to measure current speed
Profile
Uses profiling tools to find the slowest code paths
Plan
Creates a prioritized to-do list of bottlenecks to fix
Fix & Verify
Implements changes and re-runs benchmarks to confirm improvement

Example 2 — PII Security Detection

Auto-detected in PR Review

A developer adds user email to a Lambda function output. Claude Code, running on GitHub Actions, reads the Terraform infrastructure, traces data flow, detects that emails now reach an external S3 bucket shared with a partner, and posts a warning before merge.

Example 3 — Data Analysis

Point Claude at a CSV of customer data. It opens a Jupyter notebook, writes and runs cells iteratively, identifies churn patterns, and produces charts — all in one conversation.

✓ Check Your Understanding
05

Adding Context

Claude works best when it understands your project. Too little context gives wrong answers. Too much irrelevant context makes it slower and less accurate. The goal is just enough.

The /init Command

/init

Scans your entire codebase, understands the architecture, and creates a Claude.md file. This file is automatically included in every future request.

Three Types of Claude.md

TypeCommitted?Use Case
Project level✓ YesArchitecture, coding standards, key files — shared with team
Local level✗ NoPersonal preferences, notes just for you
Machine level✗ NoGlobal preferences across all your projects

Example Claude.md

## Project: E-commerce Platform
- Database schema → /db/schema.sql (always reference for DB questions)
- We use PostgreSQL, not MySQL
- Auth handled by /src/auth/middleware.ts
- Never modify files in /vendor/

The @ Symbol — Pinpoint Context

@src/auth/middleware.ts — why is the login token expiring early?

Mention files directly instead of letting Claude search. It's like handing a new employee the exact page they need instead of saying "the answer is somewhere in the wiki."

The # Symbol — Memory Mode

# Always use async/await instead of .then() chains

Immediately updates your Claude.md so Claude remembers this in all future interactions.

06

Making Changes & Power Modes

Pasting Screenshots — Ctrl+V

⚠ Mac Gotcha

Use Control+V, not Command+V. Command+V does not paste screenshots into Claude Code on macOS.

Screenshot a broken UI element, paste it, and say "fix the alignment here." No need to describe what you see in words.

Plan Mode — For Breadth

Activate with Shift + Tab (twice). Claude will research all relevant files, build a detailed plan, and only then start making changes. Ideal for multi-file features and refactoring.

Thinking Mode — For Depth

Include phrases like "Ultra think" in your message. Gives Claude an extended reasoning budget for complex logic and subtle bugs.

Ultra think — why does our payment processing occasionally fail
for orders placed exactly at midnight?

When to Use Each Mode

ModeHandlesBest For
Plan ModeBreadthMulti-step tasks, wide codebase understanding
Thinking ModeDepthTricky logic, debugging specific issues
Both combinedBreadth + DepthThe most complex tasks you face
Cost Note

Both modes consume additional tokens. Use them strategically rather than on every request.

Git Integration

Claude Code can stage files, create commits, and write descriptive commit messages automatically. Just say "commit the changes you just made."

07

Controlling Context

Long conversations accumulate noise that can confuse Claude. These tools keep you focused:

Escape ×1
Stop Claude mid-response and redirect
Escape + #
Stop, then add permanent memory to prevent repeated mistakes
Escape ×2
Rewind: jump back to any earlier message
/compact
Summarize conversation, preserve knowledge, clear clutter
/clear
Wipe entire conversation — fresh start for a new task
Pro Tip

Use Double Escape when you've gone down a debugging rabbit hole for 10+ messages with no results. Jump back to before the rabbit hole and try a different approach — without losing relevant context.

});
